([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Alma Mater Studiorum – Università di Bologna*; abbreviated **Unibo**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), Italy. It is the [oldest university in continuous operation in the world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), and the first degree-awarding institution of higher learning.[[4]](#cite_note-Hunt_Janin_2008-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Ridder-Symoens_1992-5) Teaching began around 1088, with the university becoming organised as *[universitas scholarium](/wiki/Universitas_scholarium "Universitas scholarium")* or guilds of students by the late 12th century.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The university's emblem carries the motto *Alma Mater Studiorum* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for 'Nourishing mother of studies') and the date *A.D. 1088*.[[7]](#cite_note-campuses-and-structures_schools-7) With over 90,000 students, the University of Bologna is one of the [largest universities in Europe](/wiki/List_of_largest_universities_by_enrollment "List of largest universities by enrollment").

The **University of Bologna** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Alma Mater Studiorum – Università di Bologna*; abbreviated **Unibo**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), Italy. It is the [oldest university in continuous operation in the world](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), and the first degree-awarding institution of higher learning.[[4]](#cite_note-Hunt_Janin_2008-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Ridder-Symoens_1992-5) Teaching began around 1088, with the university becoming organised as *[universitas scholarium](/wiki/Universitas_scholarium "Universitas scholarium")* or guilds of students by the late 12th century.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The university's emblem carries the motto *Alma Mater Studiorum* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for 'Nourishing mother of studies') and the date *A.D. 1088*.[[7]](#cite_note-campuses-and-structures_schools-7) With over 90,000 students, the University of Bologna is one of the [largest universities in Europe](/wiki/List_of_largest_universities_by_enrollment "List of largest universities by enrollment").

The university saw the first woman to earn a university degree and teach at a university ([Bettisia Gozzadini](/wiki/Bettisia_Gozzadini "Bettisia Gozzadini")), and the first woman to earn both a doctorate in science and a salaried position as a university professor ([Laura Bassi](/wiki/Laura_Bassi "Laura Bassi")). The University of Bologna had a central role in the sciences during the medieval age and the Italian renaissance, when it housed and educated [Nicholas Copernicus](/wiki/Nicholas_Copernicus "Nicholas Copernicus") in addition to numerous other renaissance mathematicians.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It has educated a wide range of notable alumni, amongst them a large number of Italian scientists, prime ministers, supreme court judges and priests.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Aside from its main campus in Bologna, the university has additional campuses in [Cesena](/wiki/Cesena "Cesena"), [Forlì](/wiki/Forl%C3%AC "Forlì"), [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") and [Rimini](/wiki/Rimini "Rimini"), as well as branches abroad in [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai").[[10]](#cite_note-10) It houses the fully funded boarding college *Collegio Superiore di Bologna*, the Bologna School of Advanced Studies,[[11]](#cite_note-campuses-and-structures-11) the [botanical gardens of Bologna](/wiki/Orto_Botanico_dell%27Universit%C3%A0_di_Bologna "Orto Botanico dell'Università di Bologna"), a large number of museums, libraries and archeological collections,[[12]](#cite_note-12) as well as the [Bologna University Press](/wiki/Bologna_University_Press "Bologna University Press").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Bologna&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Universit%C3%A4t_Bologna_Deutsche_Nation.jpg/250px-Universit%C3%A4t_Bologna_Deutsche_Nation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universit%C3%A4t_Bologna_Deutsche_Nation.jpg)

The entry of students in the *Natio Germanica Bononiae*, the [nation](/wiki/Nation_(university) "Nation (university)") of German students at Bologna; miniature of 1497.

The date of the University of Bologna's founding is uncertain. The university was granted a charter (*[Authentica habita](/wiki/Authentica_habita "Authentica habita")*) by [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") [Frederick I Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_I_Barbarossa "Frederick I Barbarossa") in 1158, but in the 19th century, a committee of historians led by [Giosuè Carducci](/wiki/Giosu%C3%A8_Carducci "Giosuè Carducci") traced the founding of the university back to 1088, which would make it the oldest continuously operating university in the world.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, the development of the institution at Bologna into a university was a gradual process. Paul Grendler writes that "it is not likely that enough instruction and organization existed to merit the term *university* before the 1150s, and it might not have happened before the 1180s."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The university arose around [mutual aid societies](/wiki/Mutual_aid_societies "Mutual aid societies") (known as *universitates scholarium*) of foreign students called "[nations](/wiki/Nation_(university) "Nation (university)")" (as they were grouped by nationality) for protection against city laws which imposed [collective punishment](/wiki/Collective_punishment "Collective punishment") on foreigners for the crimes and debts of their countrymen. These students then hired scholars from the city's pre-existing lay and ecclesiastical schools to teach them subjects such as liberal arts, notarial law, theology, and *ars dictaminis* ([scrivenery](/wiki/Scrivener "Scrivener")).[[17]](#cite_note-17) The lectures were given in informal schools called *scholae*. In time the various *universitates scholarium* decided to form a larger association, or *Studium*—thus, the university. The *Studium* grew to have a strong position of [collective bargaining](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") with the city, since by then it derived significant revenue through visiting foreign students, who would depart if they were not well treated. The foreign students in Bologna received greater rights, and collective punishment was ended. There was also collective bargaining with the scholars who served as professors at the university. By the initiation or threat of a [student strike](/wiki/Student_strike "Student strike"), the students could enforce their demands as to the content of courses and the pay professors would receive. University professors were hired, fired, and had their pay determined by an elected council of two representatives from every student "nation" which governed the institution, with the most important decisions requiring a majority vote from all the students to ratify. The professors could also be fined if they failed to finish classes on time, or complete course material by the end of the semester. A student committee, the "Denouncers of Professors", kept tabs on them and reported any misbehavior. Professors themselves were not powerless, however, forming *collegia doctorum* (professors' committees) in each faculty, and securing the rights to set examination fees and degree requirements. Eventually, the city ended this arrangement, paying professors from tax revenues and making it a chartered [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg/250px-Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archiginnasio_ora_blu_Bologna.jpg)

*[Archiginnasio](/wiki/Archiginnasio_of_Bologna "Archiginnasio of Bologna")*, main seat of the University between 1563 and 1803

The university is historically notable for its teaching of [canon](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") and [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(common_law) "Civil law (common law)");[[19]](#cite_note-19) indeed, it was set up in large part with the aim of studying the *[Digest](/wiki/Digest_(Roman_law) "Digest (Roman law)"),*[[20]](#cite_note-20) a central text in [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"), which had been rediscovered in Italy in 1070, and the university was central in the development of [medieval Roman law](/wiki/Medieval_Roman_law "Medieval Roman law").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Until modern times, the only degree granted at that university was the doctorate.

[Bettisia Gozzadini](/wiki/Bettisia_Gozzadini "Bettisia Gozzadini") earned a law degree in 1237, being one of the first women in history to obtain a university degree.[[22]](#cite_note-22) She taught law from her own home for two years, and in 1239 she taught at the university, becoming the first woman in history to teach at a university.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1711, [Luigi Ferdinando Marsili](/wiki/Luigi_Ferdinando_Marsili "Luigi Ferdinando Marsili") convinced [Pope Clement XI](/wiki/Pope_Clement_XI "Pope Clement XI") to provide funds to establish the [Observatory of Bologna](/wiki/Observatory_of_Bologna "Observatory of Bologna"), the first public astronomical observatory in Italy.[[24]](#cite_note-Ashworth2018-24) It was a center for research by astronomers such as [Eustachio Manfredi](/wiki/Eustachio_Manfredi "Eustachio Manfredi"), [Giovanni Battista Guglielmini](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Guglielmini "Giovanni Battista Guglielmini"), and [Eustachio Zanotti](/wiki/Eustachio_Zanotti "Eustachio Zanotti") in the 18th century, and physicists like [Guido Horn d'Arturo](/wiki/Guido_Horn_d%27Arturo "Guido Horn d'Arturo") and [Luigi Giuseppe Jacchia](/wiki/Luigi_Giuseppe_Jacchia "Luigi Giuseppe Jacchia") in the 20th century.

[Laura Bassi](/wiki/Laura_Bassi "Laura Bassi") was born into a prosperous family of Bologna and was privately educated from the age of five.[[25]](#cite_note-:52-25) Bassi's education and intellect was noticed by Prospero Lorenzini Lambertini, who became the Archbishop of Bologna in 1731 (later [Pope Benedict XIV](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XIV "Pope Benedict XIV")). Lambertini became the official patron of Bassi. He arranged for a public debate between Bassi and four professors from the University of Bologna on 17 April 1732.[[26]](#cite_note-:6-26) In 1732, Bassi, aged twenty, publicly defended her forty-nine theses on *Philosophica Studia*[[27]](#cite_note-:4-27) at the Sala degli Anziani of the [Palazzo Pubblico](/wiki/Palazzo_Pubblico "Palazzo Pubblico"). The University of Bologna awarded her a doctorate degree on 12 May.[[28]](#cite_note-:9-28) She became the first woman to receive a doctorate in science, and the second woman in the world to earn a philosophy doctorate after [Elena Cornaro Piscopia](/wiki/Elena_Cornaro_Piscopia "Elena Cornaro Piscopia") in 1678, fifty-four years prior. She was by then popularly known as Bolognese [Minerva](/wiki/Minerva "Minerva").[[29]](#cite_note-:12-29)[[25]](#cite_note-:52-25) On 29 October 1732, the Senate and the University of Bologna granted Bassi's candidature, and in December she was appointed professor of natural philosophy to teach physics.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) She became the first salaried woman lecturer in the world,[[32]](#cite_note-:10-32) thus beginning her academic career. She was also the first woman member of any scientific establishment, when she was elected to the [Academy of Sciences of the Institute of Bologna](/wiki/Academy_of_Sciences_of_the_Institute_of_Bologna "Academy of Sciences of the Institute of Bologna") in 1732.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Bassi became the most important populariser of [Newtonian mechanics](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics") in Italy.[[35]](#cite_note-:32-35)

In 1971, the Hellenist Benedetto Marzullo in company with [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco"), Renato Barilli, Adelio Ferrero instituted within the Faculty of Letters and Arts the DAMS ([acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") of *discipline delle arti, della musica e dello spettacolo*, "Discipline of [Arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts"), [Musics](/wiki/Music "Music") and [Performance](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art")"). It was the first degree course of this type to be opened in Italy. Between December 26, 1982, and November 29, 1983, there occurred the DAMS murders (in [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Delitti del DAMS*), dealing with four victims who were students or professors of the DAMS: Angelo Fabbri (a brilliant student of [Umberto Eco](/wiki/Umberto_Eco "Umberto Eco")), Liviana Rossi, the dancer Francesca Alinovi (who was stabbed for 47 times), and Leonarda Polvani.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

*Navile Campus* houses the departments of Chemistry, Industrial Chemistry, Pharmacy and Physics and Astronomy.

Higher education processes are being harmonised across the European Community. Nowadays the university offers 101 different "*[Laurea](/wiki/Laurea "Laurea")*" or "*Laurea breve*" first-level degrees (three years of courses), followed by 108 "*Laurea specialistica*" or "*Laurea magistrale*" second-level degrees (two years). However 11 other courses have maintained the previous rules of "*Laurea specialistica a ciclo unico*" or "*Laurea magistrale a ciclo unico*", with only one cycle of study of five years, except for medicine and dentistry, which require six years of courses. After the "*Laurea*" one may attain first-level Master (one-year diploma, similar to a postgraduate diploma). After second-level degrees are attained, one may proceed to second-level Master, specialisation schools (residency) or research doctorates (PhD).

The 11 Schools (which replace the existing 23 faculties) are:

* School of Agriculture and [Veterinary Medicine](/wiki/Veterinary_Medicine "Veterinary Medicine")
* School of Economics, Management and Statistics
* School of Engineering and Architecture
* School of Foreign Languages and Literature, Interpreting and Translation
* School of Law
* School of Arts, Humanities, and [Cultural Heritage](/wiki/Cultural_Heritage "Cultural Heritage")
* School of Medicine and Surgery
* School of Pharmacy, [Biotechnologies](/wiki/Biotechnologies "Biotechnologies") and Sport Sciences

## Affiliates and other institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Bologna&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Affiliates and other institutions")]

### Il Mulino

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Bologna&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Il Mulino")]

In the early 1950s, some students of the University of Bologna were among the founders of the review "il Mulino". On 25 April 1951 the first issue of the review was published in Bologna.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In a short time, "il Mulino" became one of the most interesting reference points in Italy for the political and cultural debate and established important editorial relationships in Italy and abroad. Editorial activities evolved along with the review. In 1954, the il Mulino publishing house (It. *Società editrice il Mulino*) was founded, which today represents one of the most relevant Italian publishers. In addition to this were initiated research projects (focusing mostly on the educational institutions and the political system in Italy), that eventually led, in 1964, to the establishment of the Istituto Carlo Cattaneo.

### Collegio Superiore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Bologna&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Collegio Superiore")]

The *Collegio Superiore* is an excellence institution inside the University of Bologna, aimed at promoting students' merit through dedicated learning programmes.

The institution was founded in 1998 as *Collegio d'Eccellenza*. Together with the Institute for Advanced Study it is part of the Institute for Higher Study.

The Collegio Superiore offers an additional educational path to students enrolled in a degree programme at the University of Bologna, providing specialized courses as part of an interdisciplinary framework.

All students of the Collegio Superiore are granted a full-ride scholarship and additional benefits such as the assistance of a personal tutor and free accommodation at the Residence for Higher Study. In order to remain members of the Collegio Superiore students are required to maintain high marks in both their degree programme and the additional courses.

Beatrice Fraboni, professor of Physics of Matter, has been head of Collegio Superiore since 2019.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The 2024 *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")* ranked the University of Bologna 154th in the world as well as 73rd (1st in Italy, 18th in Europe) with specific reference to academic reputation. In another measurement by the same organization, it was positioned among the world's top 100 universities for graduate employability.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In the 2024 *[Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")*, it claimed the 155th place globally.[[101]](#cite_note-101) In the 2023 *THE* Impact Rankings, which measure the universities' commitment to sustainable development in compliance with the [UN 2030 Agenda](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goals "Sustainable Development Goals"), Bologna took 5th place in Europe and 23rd in the world.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Furthermore, Bologna topped Italy's 2023 list of large public universities (> 40,000 students), produced by the Italian Center for Social Investment Studies, for the 14th year in a row.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

"There is no indication, however, that up until around 1180, the Bolognese law schools were anything other than private schools opened and run by each master after his own fashion, gathering together the students that had entered into an agreement with him and paid him fees (*collectae*) in return for his teaching. The crucial change would seem to have taken place around the years 1180–90. ... The masters, who were themselves mainly Bolognese in origin, agreed from 1189 to swear an oath to the commune not to seek to transfer the *studium* elsewhere. The students, on the other hand, began to group themselves in nations, according to their places of origin (we hear of the Lombard nation as early as 1191), and these were soon federated into 'universities' with elected rectors at their head."
